en.name: Oxygen saturation in venous blood
en.longDescription: ## Definition & Clinical Context Venous oxygen saturation is the fraction of haemoglobin in venous blood bound to oxygen, expressed as a percentage. It is measured by CO-oximetry on a venous blood sample. In critical care, it is used to assess the balance between oxygen delivery and consumption: a low venous saturation suggests inadequate oxygen delivery relative to demand (low cardiac output, anaemia, hypoxaemia) or increased extraction. The source data will typically be a single numeric result per patient-timestamp, reported as a percentage (0-100%). **Note: SvO2 vs ScvO2 vs peripheral SvO2.** Three distinct venous saturation measurements exist, with different sampling sites and clinical interpretations [1, 2]: - **SvO2 (mixed venous saturation)** — sampled from the pulmonary artery via a pulmonary artery catheter. It is the true average venous saturation of the whole body and the gold standard for oxygen delivery/consumption balance. In adults, residual mixed venous oxygen saturation is approximately 60-80% [1]. - **ScvO2 (central venous saturation)** — sampled from a central venous catheter, typically in the superior vena cava. It reflects upper-body return only, not the whole body, but is much less invasive than SvO2 and used as a surrogate. In healthy adults, ScvO2 is slightly **lower** than SvO2 [2]. In circulatory failure (heart failure, cardiogenic shock, septic shock), blood flow is redirected away from the splanchnic bed; this reverses the relationship and ScvO2 becomes **higher** than SvO2 — differences of 5-18% have been reported [2]. The Rivers early goal-directed therapy protocol for severe sepsis used ScvO2 ≥ 70% as a resuscitation target [3]; the clinical benefit of EGDT is now controversial, but ScvO2 monitoring remains widely used. - **Peripheral venous saturation** — sampled from a peripheral vein. Highly dependent on tourniquet, local perfusion and local metabolism. Of limited clinical value as a marker of systemic oxygen balance. This concept set covers all venous specimens (peripheral, mixed venous, central venous, capillary). The user must preserve the LOINC code in downstream analyses where the source-of-blood matters — values are **not interchangeable** across specimens. **Note: directly measured vs calculated.** LOINC distinguishes `Oxygen saturation in X blood` (direct CO-oximetry) from `Oxygen saturation Calculated from oxygen partial pressure` (computed from PO2 via the haemoglobin dissociation curve). Both forms are included; calculated values are less reliable when dyshaemoglobinaemias are present. ## Included Concepts The set lists 12 LOINC "Lab Test" concepts for venous oxygen saturation (cord blood excluded), grouped by specimen. ### Venous blood - *3024928 / 2711-0 — Oxygen saturation in Venous blood (LOINC)* - *3041253 / 51731-8 — Oxygen saturation Calculated from oxygen partial pressure in Venous blood (LOINC)* - *42869600 / 71845-2 — Oxygen saturation [Pure mass fraction] in Venous blood (LOINC)* - *42869596 / 71841-1 — Oxygen saturation [Pure mass fraction] Calculated from oxygen partial pressure in Venous blood (LOINC)* ### Mixed venous blood (SvO2) - *3018465 / 19224-5 — Oxygen saturation in Mixed venous blood (LOINC)* - *44786762 / 74105-8 — Oxygen saturation Calculated from oxygen partial pressure in Mixed venous blood (LOINC)* - *42869601 / 71846-0 — Oxygen saturation [Pure mass fraction] in Mixed venous blood (LOINC)* ### Central venous blood (ScvO2) - *1616373 / 97549-0 — Oxygen saturation in Central venous blood (LOINC)* ### Capillary blood - *3021901 / 2709-4 — Oxygen saturation in Capillary blood (LOINC)* - *3039986 / 51732-6 — Oxygen saturation Calculated from oxygen partial pressure in Capillary blood (LOINC)* - *42869606 / 71851-0 — Oxygen saturation [Pure mass fraction] in Capillary blood (LOINC)* - *42869597 / 71842-9 — Oxygen saturation [Pure mass fraction] Calculated from oxygen partial pressure in Capillary blood (LOINC)* **Note on units.** Recommended unit follows LOINC's `EXAMPLE_UCUM_UNITS`: **`%`** (concept 8554 — percent). The `[Pure mass fraction]` variants are dimensionless ratios between 0 and 1. ## Excluded Concepts No concepts are explicitly excluded by name. The set deliberately omits: - **Arterial blood specimens**: covered by the separate "Oxygen saturation in arterial blood" concept set. - **Cord blood specimens**: physiologically distinct from post-natal blood. - **Pulse oximetry codes**: pulse oximetry produces an SpO2 estimate that, by sensor placement, is intended to approximate arterial saturation, not venous. ## Mapping Notes - **Default concept** when the source reports a venous saturation without further specification: `3024928 / 2711-0 — Oxygen saturation in Venous blood`. - **Source explicitly says SvO2 (mixed venous, pulmonary artery catheter)**: map to `3018465 / 19224-5 — Oxygen saturation in Mixed venous blood`. - **Source explicitly says ScvO2 (central venous catheter)**: map to `1616373 / 97549-0 — Oxygen saturation in Central venous blood`. - **Source reports arterial saturation (SaO2, SpO2)**: do **not** map. Use "Oxygen saturation in arterial blood" or the pulse oximetry concept set. - **Do not confuse with**: arterial saturation (separate concept set), oxygen partial pressure (separate concept set). ## References [1] Lee CP, Bora V. Anesthesia Monitoring of Mixed Venous Saturation. In: *StatPearls*. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing. Available: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK539835/ [2] Reinhart K, Kuhn HJ, Hartog C, Bredle DL. Continuous central venous and pulmonary artery oxygen saturation monitoring in the critically ill. *Intensive Care Med*. 2004;30(8):1572-8. Available: https://doi.org/10.1007/s00134-004-2337-y [3] Rivers E, Nguyen B, Havstad S, Ressler J, Muzzin A, Knoblich B, et al. Early goal-directed therapy in the treatment of severe sepsis and septic shock. *N Engl J Med*. 2001;345(19):1368-77. Available: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa010307
